content.full_text:
October 6, 1989


Barbara Milman
Staff Counsel
Assembly Rules Committee
Senator Hotel
1121 L Street, Rm. 400
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
    Our File No. I-89-572

Dear Ms. Milman:

     You have requested confirmation of oral advice provided to
you regarding the Political Reform Act (the "Act.")17 A copy of
your incoming letter is attached.

     This letter confirms that, in the circumstances you have
described, funds received in connection with an interim hearing of
the Assembly Education Committee will be considered a gift of
funds to the Assembly Rules Committee, and will not be considered
either a gift or a contribution to any member of the Assembly, if
the funds are received in the manner described in In re Stone 3
FPPC Ops. 52 (copy enclosed).

     You have indicated that the Committee hearing has been
formally approved by the Assembly Rules Committee. One of the
Stone criteria requires that the making and use of the gift be
formalized in a resolution embodying the standards set forth in
the other criteria. It is not clear from your statement whether
this criteria has been met. If not, the Rules Committee should
adopt such a resolution.




1/    Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

Our File No. I-89-572
Page 2

•

If you have any further questions, please contact me at (916)
322-5901.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel
[illegible signature]

By: John G. McLean
Counsel, Legal Division

KED/JGM/aa

Enclosures

89-542

REPLY TO:
☐ SACRAMENTO ADDRESS
STATE CAPITOL
P.O. BOX 942849
SACRAMENTO, CA 94249-0001
(916) 445-7800

Assembly
California Legislature

COMMITTEES:
HEALTH & LONG TERM CARE
ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT
& NEW TECHNOLOGIES
EDUCATION
SUBCOMMITTEE ON
HIGHER EDUCATION
GOVERNMENTAL EFFICIENCY
& CONSUMER PROTECTION

PPPC
Oct 3 12:30 PM '89

DISTRICT OFFICES:
☐ 7415 FOSGLBERRY, SUITE B
GILROY, CA 95020
(408) 848-1481

☐ 545 J STREET, SUITE 14
LOS BANOS, CA 93635
(209) 826-6100

☐ 140 CENTRAL
SALINAS, CA 93901
(408) 422-4344

RUSTY AREIAS
ASSEMBLYMAN, TWENTY-FIFTH DISTRICT
CO-CHAIR, ASSEMBLY HIGH TECHNOLOGY CAUCUS

October 3, 1989

Katherine Donovan, General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Ms Donovan:

I am writing to confirm telephone advice you gave me on
September 26, 1989. You advised me that the Assembly Rules
Committee may accept gifts from private parties to help pay for
expenses incurred in an interim committee hearing held in San
Diego. In this instance, the funds will be used to pay a
public television station that is providing interactive
television coverage to the Assembly Education Committee and to
agencies from other states with experience or an interest in
the subject matter of the hearing. The funds will be given
directly to the Assembly Rules Committee, which will contract
with the television provider. None of the funds will be used
for the personal benefit (such as travel) of any legislator.

The Committee hearing has been formally approved by a vote of
the Assembly Rules Committee, which has authority under the
Assembly Rules to provide the funds for the hearing.

You have advised me that, under these circumstances, the gift
to the Assembly Rules Committee will not constitute either a
gift or a contribution to any member of the Assembly.

I would appreciate confirmation of this advice as soon as
possible.

Sincerely,

[signature]
BARBARA MILMAN
Staff Counsel

sections.question: Whether the Assembly Rules Committee may accept gifts from private parties to help pay for expenses incurred in an interim committee hearing held in San Diego, where the funds will be used to pay a public television station providing interactive television coverage to the Assembly Education Committee and to agencies from other states, with the funds given directly to the Assembly Rules Committee which will contract with the television provider, and none of the funds used for the personal benefit of any legislator.
sections.conclusion: In the circumstances described, funds received in connection with an interim hearing of the Assembly Education Committee will be considered a gift of funds to the Assembly Rules Committee, and will not be considered either a gift or a contribution to any member of the Assembly, if the funds are received in the manner described in In re Stone 3 FPPC Ops. 52. However, the making and use of the gift must be formalized in a resolution embodying the standards set forth in the Stone criteria, and it is not clear from the statement whether this criteria has been met. If not, the Rules Committee should adopt such a resolution.
sections.question_synthetic: Can the Assembly Rules Committee accept private gifts to fund interactive television coverage of a committee hearing, and would such gifts constitute gifts or contributions to individual Assembly members?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC confirms that private gifts to the Assembly Rules Committee for committee hearing expenses would not constitute gifts or contributions to individual Assembly members, provided the gift-making and use are formalized in a resolution meeting the criteria established in In re Stone.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the original incoming request letter from Barbara Milman (October 3, 1989) and the FPPC response letter from General Counsel Kathryn Donovan (October 6, 1989). The question and conclusion are derived from the incoming request and FPPC response respectively. The FPPC response references In re Stone 3 FPPC Ops. 52 as controlling authority. Minor uncertainty exists regarding whether all Stone criteria have been met.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the Assembly Rules Committee may accept gifts from private parties to help pay for expenses incurred in an interim committee hearing held in San Diego, where the funds will be used to pay a public television station providing interactive television coverage to the Assembly Education Committee and to agencies from other states, with the funds given directly to the Assembly Rules Committee which will contract with the television provider, and none of the funds used for the personal benefit of any legislator.

CONCLUSION: In the circumstances described, funds received in connection with an interim hearing of the Assembly Education Committee will be considered a gift of funds to the Assembly Rules Committee, and will not be considered either a gift or a contribution to any member of the Assembly, if the funds are received in the manner described in In re Stone 3 FPPC Ops. 52. However, the making and use of the gift must be formalized in a resolution embodying the standards set forth in the Stone criteria, and it is not clear from the statement whether this criteria has been met. If not, the Rules Committee should adopt such a resolution.

embedding.summary: FPPC response confirming that the Assembly Rules Committee may accept private gifts for committee hearing expenses (specifically interactive television coverage) without such gifts constituting gifts or contributions to individual Assembly members, subject to formalization of the gift-making through an appropriate resolution.
